original_abstract: We report a mechanistic investigation into alkene hydrofunctionalization reactions mediated by the photoredox catalyst 9-mesityl-10-methylacridinium (Mes-Acr+). Through direct detection of alkene cation radical intermediates, we confirm that the phenylthiyl radical rapidly oxidizes the persistent acridinyl radical, a key step that bridges the photoredox and hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) catalytic manifolds. Our results further indicate that diphenyl disulfide ((PhS)2) and thiophenol (PhSH) operate via a common catalytic pathway, initiated by the photolytic cleavage of the disulfide bond. Density functional theory (DFT) analysis of the HAT transition states reveals that PhSH possesses a significantly lower activation barrier for hydrogen atom donation compared to 2-phenylmalononitrile (PMN), attributed largely to differences in structural reorganization. During the early stages of the reaction, we observe that Mes-Acr+ undergoes off-cycle adduct formation, likely triggered by the accumulation of PhS−. Finally, observed kinetic discrepancies between PhSH and (PhS)2 as HAT sources suggest that the proton transfer step may exert a substantial influence on the overall reaction rate.
humanized_abstract: Alkene Hydrofunctionalization with Mes-Acr+ Mediated Photoredox Catalysis: Mechanistic Insights and Kinetic Investigations In the presence of hydrogen, alkene hydrofunctionalization with 9-mesityl-10-methylacridinium (Mes-Acr+ as a photoredox catalyst has been fully explored and an evidence for alkene cation radical (AR) intermediates has been observed. After studying various components, we demonstrated that the phenylthiyl radical (PhS) oxidized the persistent acridinyl radical (Ar), providing a direct link between photoredox catalysis and HAT. Furthermore, we studied the functional role of (PhS)2 and thiophenol, and found that they proceed through a similar catalytic pathway, which commences by breaking the disulfide bond through photolysis. Using DFT calculations, we compared the relative reactivities of the hydrogen atom donors (PhSH and 2-phenylmalononitrile (PMN). The results indicated that PhSH is favorable to act as a HAT agent over PMN and the lower activation energy of PhSH in relation to PMN is largely due to a lesser steric hindrance during the TS formation. We have also demonstrated that during the initial part of the catalysis, Mes-Acr+ was observed to give adducts off the main cycle which we speculated are due to PhS−. Eventually, we attributed the difference in reactivity toward hydrogen between (PhS)2 and PhSH toward hydrogen to the proton transfer step being rate limiting in the HAT cycle.
